coredata.dc:title: Simulation of thermal ablation by high-intensity focused ultrasound with temperature-dependent properties
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Huang, C.W.
Sun, M.K.
Chen, B.T.
Shieh, J.
Chen, C.S.
Chen, W.S.
coredata.dc:description: An integrated computational framework was developed in this study for modeling high-intensity focused ultrasound (HIFU) thermal ablation. The temperature field was obtained by solving the bioheat transfer equation (BHTE) through the finite element method; while, the thermal lesion was considered as a denatured material experiencing phase transformation and modeled with the latent heat. An equivalent attenuation coefficient, which considers the temperature-dependent properties of the target material and the ultrasound diffraction due to bubbles, was proposed in the nonlinear thermal transient analysis. Finally, a modified thermal dose formulation was proposed to predict the lesion size, shape and location. In-vitro thermal ablation experiments on transparent tissue phantoms at different energy levels were carried out to validate this computational framework. The temperature histories and lesion areas from the proposed model show good correlation with those from the in-vitro experiments.
coredata.dcterms:subject: High-intensity focused ultrasound (HIFU)
Ablation
Attenuation coefficient
Latent heat
Thermal dose

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic of the prototype HIFU system. CH1 of the oscilloscope is the signal-receiving channel and Ext is connected with the Sync of the function generator for trigger setting. (b) Relative positions of the HIFU transducer, target sample and thermocouples.
Evolutions of acoustic attenuation coefficients of NIPAM-based hydrogel phantom and air with temperature.

Levels of material damage induced in NIPAM-based hydrogel phantoms under different combinations of HIFU acoustic power and ablation duration. The upper and lower boundary lines indicate the approximate thresholds of inducing permanent and transient bubbles, respectively.
Pseudo attenuation coefficient versus temperature for 57.93 and 82.7W electrical powers at 2MHz.
Equivalent attenuation coefficient versus temperature for 57.93 and 82.7W electrical powers at 2MHz.
Lesions produced in phantom specimens in HIFU ablation experiments under four different electrical powers: (a) 25.0W, (b) 41.7W, (c) 57.9W and (d) 82.7W.

Temperature history curves at different measuring points in hydrogel phantom obtained from HIFU ablation experiments and corresponding FEM simulations under four different electrical powers: (a) 25.0W, (b) 41.7W, (c) 57.9W and (d) 82.7W.

Lesions obtained from FEM simulations of HIFU ablation of hydrogel phantom using the equivalent attenuation coefficient, αeq , and modified thermal dose, TD 52, under four different electrical powers: (a) 25.0W, (b) 41.7W, (c) 57.9W and (d) 82.7W.
Properties of NIPAM-based hydrogel phantom at different temperatures.
Conditions of four HIFU ablation experiments of NIPAM-based hydrogel phantom.
Values of parameters in the pseudo attenuation coefficient for high electrical powers.
Lesion areas in NIPAM-based hydrogel phantom obtained from ablation experiments and corresponding simulations (unit: cm2).

head.ce:title: Simulation of thermal ablation by high-intensity focused ultrasound with temperature-dependent properties

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Civil Engineering, Chung Yuan Christian University, Taoyuan, Taiwan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physical Medicine and Rehabilitation, National Taiwan University Hospital & College of Medicine, Taipei, Taiwan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Physical Medicine and Rehabilitation
National Taiwan University Hospital & College of Medicine

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Division of Medical Engineering Research, National Health Research Institutes, Miaoli, Taiwan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Materials Science and Engineering, National Taiwan University, Taipei, Taiwan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: e
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Civil Engineering, National Taiwan University, Taipei, Taiwan

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Department of Civil Engineering, Chung Yuan Christian University, Taoyuan 32023, Taiwan (C.W. Huang). Department of Physical Medicine and Rehabilitation, National Taiwan University Hospital, Taipei, Taiwan (W.S. Chen).

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An integrated computational framework was developed in this study for modeling high-intensity focused ultrasound (HIFU) thermal ablation. The temperature field was obtained by solving the bioheat transfer equation (BHTE) through the finite element method; while, the thermal lesion was considered as a denatured material experiencing phase transformation and modeled with the latent heat. An equivalent attenuation coefficient, which considers the temperature-dependent properties of the target material and the ultrasound diffraction due to bubbles, was proposed in the nonlinear thermal transient analysis. Finally, a modified thermal dose formulation was proposed to predict the lesion size, shape and location. In-vitro thermal ablation experiments on transparent tissue phantoms at different energy levels were carried out to validate this computational framework. The temperature histories and lesion areas from the proposed model show good correlation with those from the in-vitro experiments.

ce:section.ce:para: The intention of a high-intensity focused ultrasound (HIFU) treatment is to raise the temperature of a targeted, isolated tissue volume above 55°C to generate coagulative necrosis and tumor death. An important feature of HIFU treatments is that the damage is spatially confined with no surrounding tissue damage. Ultrasonic energy absorption within the focal volume induces high temperatures at the focus, while, temperature outside the focal region maintains at levels of not cytotoxic. The volume of destroyed tissue within the focal region, resulted from bioeffects of thermal ablation and cavitation, is referred to as a “lesion”.

HIFU has become a potential alternative to conventional therapies for primary and metastatic tumors, especially for those patients who are not suitable candidates for surgical resection due to certain criteria, such as old age, poor health condition, multiple tumors, tumor size, or tumor location with respect to a key vessel [1–6]. HIFU has many advantages including being non-invasive, having a large penetration depth, lower operating cost, superior selectiveness and easier power control when compared with other physical methods for tissue ablation, such as lasers, microwaves or radio frequency (RF) fields.

Modern HIFU devices operate at very high focal intensity levels from several thousand W/cm2 to greater than 25,000W/cm2 in situ and concentrate the ultrasound energy on to a millimeter- or even sub-millimeter-sized focal spot. In practice, thousands of focal spots might be needed to cover the entire volume of a tumor. Thorough understanding of HIFU characteristics is therefore important for both the accurate prediction of ultrasound-induced bioeffects in tissues and the development of standards to ensure the safety and efficacy of thermal treatments.

Numerical simulations of thermal ablation aim to solve the physical equations governing energy deposition and heat transfer in tissues in order to determine the transient temperature profiles and assess tissue damages during thermal therapies. The results of simulations are useful for prototyping and optimizing the design of device geometries. In addition, they also form the basis of treatment planning platforms which assist physicians in tailoring thermal therapy procedures and operating parameters [7–10]. Comprehensive modeling of thermal ablation is essential to fully understand the physical properties of tissues which impact the energy deposition and heat transfer in them. It is worth noting that some of these properties may change substantially during heating. Several studies have reported the impact of heating on the material properties of various tissues, in both in-vivo and ex-vivo tissue models [11–14]. The power deposition and heating pattern may be affected by the variations of material properties.

Despite the literature data showing dynamic changes in tissue properties during heating, most numerical simulations of thermal ablations in the literature did not include all of the relevant changes in tissue properties. In some studies, constant tissue properties were adopted during thermal ablation to study the region of thermal coagulation [15–24]. There have been few studies analysing the cumulative impact of all relevant parameters for modeling thermal ablation with ultrasonic sources [25,26]. In order to accurately estimate the lesion formation, it is essential to perform a comparative and quantitative analysis of dynamic changes in tissue physical properties.

As the amplitude of sound waves or the exposure time of HIFU thermal therapies increases, small cavities, which contain vapor as well as some of the gas originally dissolved in the surrounding medium, can occur and cause significant destruction of tumor cells and tissue planes. In a HIFU thermal treatment, the region over which cavitation occurs would have increased absorption and attenuation coefficients. In addition, the presence of bubbles would result in a change in the acoustic impedance of the tissue volume, yielding larger reflection coefficients at the boundaries of the cavitation region. Although the main mechanism of cavitation by ultrasound exposure has been widely acknowledged, problems still exist in the quantitative analysis of cavitation effects. A feasible numerical model which systematically takes into account the cavitation effects when calculating the transient temperature profile does not yet exist. One reason for the difficulties in analysing cavitation effects is that cavitation cannot be easily observed and measured in real-time for in-vivo and ex-vivo tissues. Since the formation of cavitation and/or permanent bubbles is typical in clinical HIFU, the transparent, reusable tissue-mimicking hydrogel phantom system is a useful tool for studying the interaction between bubbles and lesion formation in real-time during high-power short-exposure HIFU ablations. Our previous study [27] indicated that the material properties, including mechanical and thermal properties, of the N-isopropylacrylamide (NIPAM)-based hydrogel phantom are similar to those of biological tissues. Therefore, the in-vitro hydrogel phantom ablation experiments are sufficient to represent the in-vivo and ex-vivo ablation experiments.

The objective of this paper is to develop an integrated numerical model which takes into account the dynamic changes of material properties and cavitation effects to simulate the lesion formation in hydrogel phantoms during thermal ablation. A three-dimensional finite element (FE) model is built using the commercial FE software ANSYS with two plug-in programs that are implemented to calculate the heat sources from HIFU and the thermal descriptor to estimate lesion formation. In addition, an equivalent attenuation coefficient, which consists of the attenuation of the hydrogel phantom and pseudo attenuation of the cavitation effects, is proposed with temperature-dependent characteristics. Furthermore, a modified thermal dose formulation for the hydrogel phantom is proposed to predict the size and location of the thermal lesion. The proposed computational frame work is verified by thermal ablation experiments on tissue-mimicking hydrogel phantoms.

To extend potential applications and avoid in-vivo experiments or human experimentations, in-vitro experiments are of critical importance for all ablative devices during preclinical development and surgical planning. A NIPAM-based reusable, transparent tissue-mimicking phantom [27], which is able to demonstrate the process and extent of thermal lesion formation in real time, was used in this study. The reusable hydrogel phantoms with a cloud point temperature of 52°C were fabricated for the HIFU ablation experiments. Heating the NIPAM-based hydrogel phantom past the cloud point would lead to the segregation of NIPAM, resulting in an increase in the opacity of the phantom. Moreover, the acoustic properties of the phantom are similar to those of human tissues [27,28], and its temperature and optical properties, including the cloud point and transparency, can be easily manipulated by the amount of acrylic acid (AAc) within the phantom.

The fabrication process consisted of the following steps: 0.44ml of AAc (99.5%) was dissolved in 150ml of degassed water. Next, 9g of NIPAM was added to the aqueous solution, which was gently stirred at room temperature until complete dissolution of NIPAM. 0.375g of N,N′-methylenebisacrylamide (MBAm; 97%) and 0.195g of ammonium persulfate (APS), which acted as the initiator for crosslinking, were then added consecutively into the aqueous solution. The mixture was gently stirred at room temperature until homogenized. Finally, 0.4ml of polymerization agent N,N,N′,N′-tetramethyl-ethylene (TEMED; 99%) was added to the mixture. The final aqueous mixture was immediately poured into molding containers (with dimensions of 5×5×6cm3) and allowed to polymerize completely at room temperature to form hydrogel phantoms. The hydrogel phantoms prepared were either tested in the ablation experiments within 24h after complete polymerization, or stored in an airtight container to avoid dehydration (if left in air) or swelling (if placed in water) for later experimental usage. The NIPAM-based hydrogel phantoms were optically transparent, gelatinous materials. Their material properties at room temperature are listed in Table 1 . A previous study demonstrated that the material properties of the phantoms are similar to those of human tissues [27,29].

The reasons for tissue property changes in thermal therapies include the consequences of bubbles resulting from boiling and cavitation and of biochemical processes. The former would cause voids and mechanical disruption of the tissues, and the latter could include protein denaturation and coagulation [11]. To measure the temperature-dependent material properties of the NIPAM-based hydrogel phantom, the phantom specimens were placed in a constant-temperature water tank for 30min to achieve thermal equilibrium before carrying out the following characterization tests. The water tank temperature was adjusted from 25 to 65°C in the repeated tests to study the temperature effect.

The speed of sound in the hydrogel phantom was determined by measuring the time taken for a single broadband ultrasound pulse to traverse a certain length of the phantom between two transducers, one acting as the pulse transmitter and the other as the receiver. The transducers used were 1-MHz, single-element flat-face transducers of a diameter 38mm (A392S, Panametrics). The acoustic attenuation coefficient of the phantom at 1MHz was obtained by comparing the logarithm of the spectral magnitude of the ultrasound signals traversing through a 60-mm-thick phantom specimen and a water path of the same length between the transmitting and receiving transducers. The density of the phantom was determined by the standard Archimedes’ immersion technique [30]. These three physical quantities were measured at different temperatures and the results are listed in Table 2 . Only the acoustic attenuation coefficient was determined to be temperature sensitive, while the variation of the speed of sound and the density at different temperatures were less obvious. As a result, only the acoustic attenuation coefficient was taken into account as a temperature-dependent characteristic when performing the numerical simulations.

The forming of a lesion during thermal therapy could be interpreted as a denaturation process involving a phase transformation [31]. Part of the input ultrasound energy in HIFU ablation is consumed without an increase in temperature during the formation of thermal lesions, i.e., latent heat of phase transformation. In this study, the latent heat of the NIPAM-based hydrogel phantom was measured by a differential scanning calorimeter (DSC; Q200, TA Instruments) from 10 to 95°C with a heating rate of 10°C/min [32] and was determined to be 497.8Jg−1.

The prototype HIFU system designed for the present study is shown in Fig. 1 a. A 2MHz sinusoidal waveform produced by a function generator (33120A, Agilent), in pulsed or continuous mode, was passed through a radio-frequency amplifier (150A250, Amplifier Research), a power meter (4025, Bird Electronic Corp.), and then a diplexer (RDX-2, Ritec) before being fed into a 2MHz focused piezoelectric transducer (SU-101, Sonic Concepts). The diameter of the transducer was 35mm and the radius of curvature was 55mm. The −3dB contour of the focal zone measured by a needle hydrophone (SPEH-S-0500, ONDA) was 4.5mm in length and 0.5mm in width. The ultrasound produced by the transducer was aimed at a target sample (i.e., NIPAM-based hydrogel phantom) fixed in a 15×15×23cm3 degassed water tank. The temperature of the water tank was set to 26°C during the thermal ablation experiments. The focal spot of the ultrasound was positioned inside the target sample. The echo signal of the incident ultrasound, collected by the same piezoelectric transducer, was monitored by an oscilloscope (LT354ML, LeCroy) and processed by a fast analog-to-digital (A/D) converter (PCI-9820, Adlink Technology Inc.). The spatial position of the transducer was controlled by a dual axis stepper motor (Jubilee Technology Corp.) to an accuracy of 0.1mm. Temperature near the HIFU focal spot was measured by a thermocouple data acquisition system (USB-9211A, National Instruments Taiwan). All components of the prototype HIFU system were controlled and synchronized by the LabVIEW program (National Instruments Taiwan).

For the HIFU ablation experiment, a continuous wave with an average power density of ∼2.1kWcm−2 in the focal spot (typically produced from 10 to 12MPa peak positive pressure with 60W electrical power [33]) was delivered by the piezoelectric transducer. Thermocouples recorded the evolution of temperature with time near the ultrasound focal spot during the experiment. In order to prevent the temperature measurements from being influenced by the incident ultrasound, and also to establish a reasonable distance to allow the quantification of thermal conduction time, the thermocouples were positioned 3–7mm away from the focal spot. The relative positions of the piezoelectric transducer, target sample and thermocouples are shown in Fig. 1b. The prototype HIFU system was also utilized to ultrasonically image the target sample. For imaging operations, low-intensity sinusoidal waves in a 5kHz burst rate (3–5 cycles) were applied to the target sample and the echo signals were monitored by the same transducer and processed with a sampling rate of 120MHz. Any change in the material properties of the target sample would give rise to changes in the peak position and intensity of the echo spectrum.

For the HIFU ablation experiment, the NIPAM-based hydrogel phantom specimen, with dimensions of 5×5×6cm3, was suspended in a water tank by a custom fixture at 26°C, and after temperature equilibrium was reached, the specimen was heated by the HIFU system for 120s. Four different electrical powers of the HIFU transducer (25.0, 41.7, 57.9 and 82.7W) were used separately in the thermal ablation experiments. The corresponding acoustic powers and the peak pressures of these electrical powers are listed in Table 3 . In addition, the formation of lesions by the heating process was continuously monitored by both a video camera and a portable diagnostic ultrasound system (Titan, SonoSite) with a linear array (L38 broadband, 5–10MHz).

The temperature histories at different positions close to the focal spot were recorded for the ablation experiments. Because the hydrogel phantom did not give rise to a backscattered signal when the ultrasound passed through, by using the plastic fixture frame (to fix the phantom specimen in the water tank) and thermocouples as the positional references (from their large backscattered signals), the ultrasound focal spot could be positioned at the center of the cuboid specimen and at a distance of about a few millimeters away from the thermocouples. The exact positions of the thermocouples relative to the focal spot were determined off-line by analysing the camera images.

The temperature distribution in a phantom during thermal therapies has often been computed using a bioheat transfer equation [34]:
(1)
ρ C
∂ T
∂ t
= k
∇
2
T +
w
b
C
b
(
T
b
- T ) + q
where T is the tissue temperature, which is a spatial and temporal function, t is the time, and ρ, C, and k are the density, specific heat capacity and thermal conductivity of the tissue, respectively. wb is the blood perfusion rate in the heated region, Cb is the specific heat capacity of blood, and Tb is the temperature of the arterial blood. In the absence of perfusion and any metabolic processes, the second term on the right hand side of Eq. (1) can be neglected. Finally, q is the rate of heat production per unit volume. The heat source, q, is equivalent to the absorbed HIFU power in the tissue, which can be obtained from [18]:
(2)
q = α
pp
∗
ρ c
where c and α are the sound speed and attenuation coefficient in tissue, respectively, and p is the acoustic pressure generated by the HIFU transducer. The superscript * denotes the complex conjugate. The heat source varies with temperature since the attenuation coefficient is temperature-dependent as described above.

From the classical theory of sound for a linear and non-dissipative medium, the acoustic pressure p of the observed point can be obtained from [35]:
(3)
p =
∫
S
a
i ω ρ A
4 π r
e
- ikr
dS
where Sa and ω represent the surface area and operation frequency of the HIFU transducer, respectively; A is the complex number of the point source intensity, which can be interpreted as the amplitude of the simulated pressure; r is the distance between the observed point and the point source; and
i =
- 1
. The pressure amplitude A is determined by fitting the experimental data. In Eq. (3), it is assumed that the calculated acoustic pressure is independent of temperature, which implies that the acoustic pressure field and temperature field are not coupled.

The temperature-dependent characteristic of the acoustic attenuation coefficient of the NIPAM-based hydrogel phantom was fitted by a regressive formula, which extrapolated experimental data up to 75°C. In addition, the acoustic attenuation coefficient of the hydrogel phantom should not exceed that of air, although it increased with increasing temperature before 75°C. As a result, the acoustic attenuation coefficient of the phantom was set equal to that of air after 75°C and as a constant after 100°C. Fig. 2 shows the temperature-dependent attenuation coefficients of the hydrogel phantom and air [36] with temperature. The corresponding regressive formula for the attenuation coefficient of the hydrogel phantom is written as:
(4)
α
m
=
- 3.748 ×
10
- 5
T
3
+ 6.044 ×
10
- 3
T
2
- 2.390 ×
10
- 1
T + 3.308
for
25
° C ⩽ T ⩽ 75
° C
5.667 ×
10
- 3
T + 3.118
for
75
° C ⩽ T ⩽ 100
° C
3.6573
for
T > 100
° C
where the subscript m signifies that the observed variation in the attenuation coefficient with temperature.

The existence of bubbles plays an important role in HIFU ablation and can be useful in certain cases [37,38]. The formation of bubbles, resulting from boiling and cavitation, may be the key reason for the formation of tadpole-shaped lesions in target tissues. Moreover, bubbles may act as scatters, which help to identify the position of the HIFU focus or thermal lesion in an acoustically transparent hydrogel phantom. Sun et al. [35] showed that HIFU ablation at high electrical powers could lead to the formation of transient or permanent bubbles at the focal region in the NIPAM-based phantoms. The energy thresholds for inducing different types of bubbles in the phantoms during HIFU ablation were investigated. Fig. 3 shows that the thresholds for the transient and permanent bubbles can be expressed in terms of the electrical power and ablation time as:
(5a)
I
lb
= - 7.116 ln ( t ) + 60.13
(5b)
I
ub
= - 4.243 ln ( t ) + 54.81
where I and t represent the electrical power and ablation time, respectively, and the subscripts lb and ub represent the lower and upper bounds, respectively. Eq. (5) indicates that for a higher electrical power, a shorter ablation time is needed to generate bubbles in the hydrogel phantom. In our ablation experiments, transient bubbles existed when the electrical power exceeded the lower bound as described in Eq. (5a); while, permanent bubbles existed when the electrical power exceeded the upper bound as described in Eq. (5b). Fig. 3 shows that for the phantom ablation experiments, bubbles did not exist under 25.0 and 41.7W of electrical powers (corresponding to 15 and 25W of acoustic powers, respectively) for 120s.

In this study, the effects of bubbles in lesion formation during HIFU ablation were taken into account by increasing the attenuation coefficient of the phantom. The pseudo attenuation coefficient, resulting from the bubble effects, is described using NACA’s 4-digit symmetrical airfoil equation [39] in terms of temperature, which is written as:
(6a)

where αc and αh are intermediate parameters and x is interpreted as the normalized temperature. M and H represent the maximum camber for αc and αh , respectively, and M is set to be H/2 in this study for simplicity. As a result, xc and H are the only two undetermined parameters: they represent the location of the normalized temperature corresponding to the maximum camber and the maximum value of αh , respectively.

Eqs. (6) and (7) imply that the pseudo attenuation coefficient diminishes to zero at 25 and 75°C and remains zero outside the temperature range of 25–75°C. The unknown parameters, xc and H, were determined through fitting the FE simulated lesion area and temperature-history curves with experimental measurements. For instance, temperature increases rapidly under high electrical powers; this corresponds to a small xc and a large H. As a result, the equivalent attenuation coefficient αeq of the phantom in FE simulations can be written as:
(8)
α
eq
=
α
m
+
α
pseudo
where the first and second terms on the right-hand side represent, respectively, the contributions from the phantom material and bubble effects. Figs. 4 and 5
show, respectively, the evolutions of the pseudo and equivalent attenuation coefficients with temperature for HIFU ablation under 57.9 and 82.7W electrical powers. The values of xc and H corresponding to 57.9 and 82.7W electrical powers are listed in Table 4 .

The original concept of thermal dose is based on that the effect of thermal therapy and can be summarized by the relationship between the therapy acting time and external temperature of the target cells (i.e., therapy temperature) [40]. The temperature-time history for a particular tissue volume is integrated and reduced to an equivalent exposure time at 43°C. For temperatures achieved during the thermal therapy by HIFU, the expression for thermal dose can be written as:

(9)
TD
43
=
∫
t =
t
0
t =
t
end
R
( 43 - T ( t ) )
dt ≈
∑
t =
t
0
t =
t
end
R
( 43 - T ( t ) )
Δ t
where t 0 and tend are the starting and ending time of the sonication, and R, known as the iso-dose constant, is 0.5 for temperatures higher than 43°C and 0.25 for temperatures lower than 43°C. Once the thermal dose exceeds a threshold, the target tissue is viewed as denatured.

To measure the thermal dose threshold for the NIPAM-based hydrogel phantom, we designed a water bathing experiment, in which room-temperature phantom specimens (with dimensions of 1×1×1cm3) were submerged in a high, constant temperature water tank. Changes in the specimen color throughout the heating process were monitored by a high-definition video (HDV) camera (HDR-XR150, SONY) at 30 frames per second. The average grayscale pixel value of the specimen at a particular time was calculated as a measure of the optical transparency of the phantom specimen to determine if the specimen was denatured. The image frames recorded for the heating process were processed off-line by an in-house MATLAB program.

Our experiments demonstrated that the hydrogel phantom did not denature (i.e., optically from transparent to opaque) below the cloud point, a transition temperature around 52±2°C, even if the threshold thermal dose was reached with a long heating duration. Furthermore, the time for denaturation, determined from the gray-scale of the optical images, for the hydrogel phantom in 55 and 65°C water tanks were 163.75 and 83.75s, respectively. However, according to Eq. (9), the corresponding thermal doses for 55 and 65°C are 6.7×105 and 3.5×108 s, respectively. These unmatched results imply that the empirical parameters and experience-based assumptions for the expansion of the thermal dose concept in Eq. (9) may not be suitable or accurate for all ranges of temperature and tissues.

Based on the concept of Eq. (9), the temperature-time history for the hydrogel phantom is integrated and reduced to an equivalent exposure time at 52°C. The expression for thermal dose can then be modified as:
(10)
TD
52
≈
∑
t =
t
0
t =
t
end
R
( 52 - T ( t ) )
Δ t ,
for
T ( t ) ⩾ 52
° C
where the iso-dose constant, R, is set to be 0.937 to fit the results of water bath experiments for the hydrogel phantom, and subscript 52 represents the equivalent exposure time at 52°C. The threshold of the modified thermal dose, TD 52, for the hydrogel phantom in thermal ablation was determined to be about 196s.

To further investigate the temperature-dependent properties of the hydrogel phantom in the HIFU ablation experiments, a three-dimensional FE model was built using the commercial FE software ANSYS (2014, ANSYS, Inc.). The FE model was constructed to contain 1,117,286 tetrahedron elements (SOLID70 in ANSYS) and 185,413 nodes with fine mesh near the HIFU focal spot so that the behaviors of the focal spot at the centroid of the NIPAM-based hydrogel phantom could be accurately simulated. The peak pressure, p max, occurring with the electrical power, I, at the focal point can be calculated from the following equation:
(11)
p
max
=
p
0
I
I
0
where p 0 and I 0 are the reference peak pressure and reference electrical power, which are set to 10MPa and 60W [33], respectively. The peak pressure is used to determine the point source intensity, A, in Eq. (3) and then the corresponding pressure distribution in the phantom can be obtained according to Eq. (3).

The kernel of the numerical simulations for the HIFU ablation experiments was solving the bioheat transfer equation; this was completed by the FE software ANSYS. Since the heat source, q in Eq. (2), is a function of temperature, a nonlinear transient thermal analysis was carried out to solve the bioheat transfer equation in the thermal ablation simulations using ANSYS. Two plug-in programs written by TCL (Tool Command Language) were implemented to calculate the corresponding heat source as a pre-process and the modified thermal dose as a post-process at each time step in the nonlinear thermal analysis, respectively. Notice that in order to capture the effect of phase transformation of the hydrogel phantom resulted from denaturation at the cloud point temperature, the specific heat C (J/(kgK)) of the phantom was defined as a function of temperature and expressed as:
(12)
C =
3431
for
T < 50
° C
104 , 580
for
50
° C ⩽ T ⩽ 54
° C
3431
for
T > 54
° C

The denaturation of the phantom was assumed to occur in the temperature range of 50–54°C, and the corresponding specific heat in this range was calculated from the measured thermal properties of the hydrogel phantom, such as the latent heat (497.8Jg−1) and specific heats before and after denaturation. The latent heat was considered as the increase in the area under the specific heat-temperature curve from 50 to 54°C.

The HIFU ablation experiments on the NIPAM-based hydrogel phantoms were carried out in a water tank set to 26°C at four different electrical powers – 25.0, 41.7, 57.9 and 82.7W. Fig. 6 (a)–(d) shows, respectively, the optical images of the ablated phantom specimens containing the thermocouples and thermal lesions at these four electrical powers. It is evident that the lesion size increases with increasing electrical power of the HIFU transducer. A visible spindle-shape lesion was found at the lowest applied electrical power (25.0W); while, a tadpole-shape lesion was observed at the highest power (82.7W). Bubbles generated while the HIFU is acting might block the incident acoustic waves, so that the upper part of the lesion is larger in size than the lower part at high electrical powers.

The temperatures of the nodes on the outer surfaces of the FE model were set to be 26°C, i.e., the outer surfaces were insulated. In addition, the initial temperatures of all nodes in the FE model were also set to be 26°C based on the water tank temperature in the HIFU ablation experiments. Fig. 7 (a)–(d) shows comparisons between the experimentally measured and FE-simulated temperature-time curves of the hydrogel phantom at a distance of 3–7mm away from the ultrasound focal spot under four different electrical powers. One can observe that the peak temperature and the temperature increase rate become larger with increasing electrical power. In addition, the temperature history curves at the measuring points obtained from the FE analyses (dashed lines in Fig. 7(a) and (b)) agree well with those from the thermocouple measurements (solid lines in Fig. 7(a) and (b)) under low electrical powers (25 and 41.7W). The small difference in peak temperature between the measured and simulated temperature history curves may indicate that the ultrasound beam pattern in the phantom is not as perfect as the pattern modeled in the theoretical simulations, and the positions of the thermocouple measuring points relative to the HIFU focal spot are difficult to identify precisely in the phantom specimen.

As for high electrical powers (57.9 and 82.7W), comparing with the experimentally measured temperature history curves (solid lines in Fig. 7(c) and (d)), the simulated curves from the FE analyses without considering the bubble effects (dashed lines in Fig. 7(c) and (d)) are evidently much lower. According to Eq. (5), bubble formation occurs during HIFU ablation. In contrast, the simulated temperature history curves from the FE analyses upon considering the bubble effects (dotted lines in Fig. 7(c) and (d)) by adopting the equivalent attenuation coefficient coincide well with those from the experimental thermocouple measurements. It is evident from Fig. 7 that the influence of bubble effects decreases rapidly with increasing distance between the measuring point and HIFU focal point. Furthermore, for measuring points closer to the focal point, one can find that the trend of the curvature of the ascending part of the temperature history curves matches that of the equivalent attenuation coefficient-vs.-temperature curves. For example, the curvatures of the ascending part of the temperature history curves from experimental data for point 1 in Fig. 7(a) and (d) are positive and negative, respectively, which are the same as those of the equivalent attenuation coefficient-vs.-temperature curves in Figs. 2 and 5 between 25 and 75°C (for 82.7 electrical power). In other words, the curvature of the temperature history curve in the ascending part is an indicator of the existence of bubbles in the HIFU ablation experiments.

For soft tissues or hydrogel phantoms, the acoustic attenuation coefficient increases with increasing temperature to a value 2–3 time its nominal value at room temperature [11,12,41]. According to Eq. (2), an increase in the acoustic attenuation coefficient will result in an increase in the volumetric heat rate generated by HIFU thermal ablation. As a result, numerical simulations adopting a constant acoustic attenuation coefficient at a fixed power are unable to predict accurately the thermal ablation results at other operation powers. In this study, the variation of the acoustic attenuation coefficient with temperature has been introduced in the thermal ablation simulation. The temperature history curves from the numerical simulations which consider the temperature-dependent attenuation coefficient due to material denaturation showed good correlations with the experimental data under low HIFU operation powers (see Fig. 7(a) and (b)). In contrast, the correlations at high HIFU operation powers were weaker due to the presence of a large amount of cavitation bubbles at the HIFU focal region during the high-power ablation experiments. Indeed, the presence of cavitation bubbles at high HIFU operation powers has been confirmed by the optical or ultrasound images [29], and is the dominate factor in increasing heating efficiency of thermal ablation [37,42]. For the sake of simplicity, the effects of the bubble content, bubble scattering, and bubble dynamic behavior in a medium are integrated into the concept of pseudo attenuation coefficient. The model proposed in this study provides a better comprehension of the mechanism of temperature distribution and lesion development in HIFU ablation, and is expected to be useful in optimizing thermal treatment parameters without having in-vivo experiments.

The expansion of the thermal dose concept from cell to tissue and from cell external temperature to tissue internal temperature has been widely implemented in existing studies on thermal therapy. However, the values of parameters in the original thermal dose expression are debatable for different ranges of temperature and tissues. Particularly when the temperature is higher than 43°C, the thermal dose increases with a power law of 2. Since the temperature increases rapidly under the HIFU operation, the corresponding thermal dose may be overestimated and very sensitive to the time increment in calculation according to Eq. (9). In the existing literature, the thermal dose threshold for coagulative necrosis was of the range 100min< TD 43 <1000min [16–20,43,44]. The wide range implies the uncertainty of thermal dose when used as a damage index.

In this study, a modified thermal dose formulation is proposed, which provides a consistent threshold to predict the lesion formulation and is not sensitive to the time increment in calculating the accumulated thermal dose. Fig. 8 (a)–(d) demonstrates the thermal lesions obtained from the FE simulations adopting the equivalent attenuation coefficient, αeq , and modified thermal dose, TD 52, for the hydrogel phantom under four different electrical powers. At high electrical powers, an oval-shaped lesion is predicted and the upper part of the lesion is larger in size than the lower part. The non-symmetric pattern of lesion formation might indicate that the pressure distribution is not symmetric and the pressure is larger in the near field than in the far field. The lesion areas from the ablation experiments and numerical simulations are listed in Table 5 . One can find that the estimated lesion areas, based on the modified thermal dose TD 52, from the proposed computational framework are close to those from the ablation experiments. The relative errors of the lesion areas from numerical simulations are less than 5.2% in these thermal ablation experiments.

In this study, an integrated computational framework was developed to simulate the lesion formation in the NIPAM-based hydrogel phantom by HIFU ablation. The developed framework uses the classical theory of sound for a linear and non-dissipative medium to calculate the acoustic pressure field, and then solves a bioheat transfer equation which takes into account the temperature-dependent characteristics through the finite element method. In addition, an equivalent attenuation coefficient which considers the material property changes and bubble effects due to temperature variation was proposed. With the aid of the pseudo attenuation coefficient, one can discuss the contribution of bubbles in thermal ablation. Furthermore, a modified thermal dose formulation capable of explaining the biomechanics of thermal denaturation more appropriately was proposed to fit the observations in the HIFU ablation experiments of the NIPAM-based hydrogel phantom.

The formation of bubbles within the hydrogel phantom during HIFU thermal ablation can be predicted by the energy thresholds defined in this work (see Eqs. (5a) and (5b)). For low HIFU operation powers or short ablation time without the formation of bubbles, our numerical results demonstrate that the simulated temperature history curves and lesion shape and size are in good agreement with the experimental results, upon considering the temperature-dependent attenuation coefficient due to material denaturation. On the other hand, for high HIFU operation powers or long ablation time, the bubble effects on the lesion formation could be analyzed quantitatively by adjusting the pseudo attenuation coefficient, which has a particularly large influence on the effective (equivalent) attenuation coefficient. The temperature history curves and lesion areas predicted by the numerical simulations agree well with those obtained from the experimental HIFU measurements when the temperature-dependent characteristics due to biochemical processes and bubbles (from boiling and cavitation) are taken into account in the phantom ablation simulations. By comparing the simulated and experimental results, the relative deviations on the lesion area and peak temperature in the temperature history curve are less than 5.2% and 6.3%, respectively. Nevertheless, the relationship between the undetermined parameters for the pseudo attenuation coefficient (i.e., xc and H in Eq. (6)) and HIFU operation power requires further investigation in order to capture the contribution of bubbles in thermal ablation more accurately.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the financial support of the National Health Research Institutes (ME-101-PP-03) and the Ministry of Science and Technology (103-2221-E-033-025-), Republic of China (Taiwan). The authors also wish to acknowledge Dr. Shen-Yeh Chen’s assistance (at FEA-Opt Technology, Taiwan) on the finite element analyses related to this research.
